_comment: Global pattern — applies to every project that uses an LLM provider. Captures the env-var-precedence-chain convention so any committed config is safe to publish.
title: Never store LLM provider tokens in CLAUDE.md or committed configs
description: Every LLM provider token (OpenAI, Anthropic, NVIDIA NIM, OpenRouter, Groq, Google AI Studio, Cerebras, etc.) is supplied through an environment variable. CLAUDE.md, AGENTS.md, README files, example configs, and any file checked into git must never contain a literal key, token, or bearer string. Config files that consume the key reference it by env-var name only (e.g. `OPENAI_API_KEY`). The runtime resolves the key with a strict precedence chain: env var > host secrets manager > config file (env-var name only) > interactive prompt. There is no fourth fallback that reads from a checked-in file.
example:
Env-var precedence chain (highest to lowest):

  1. OPENAI_API_KEY in the process environment
  2. Secrets manager (Vault / 1Password / Doppler / Fly secrets / Vercel env)
  3. Config file that names the env var:
        { "provider": "openai", "api_key_env": "OPENAI_API_KEY" }
  4. Interactive prompt at startup (dev-only, never in production)

Never:
  { "provider": "openai", "api_key": "sk-proj-abc123..." }

Never in CLAUDE.md / README / comments:
  # Set OPENAI_API_KEY=sk-proj-abc123... before running

Do this instead:
  # Export OPENAI_API_KEY before running (see .env.example for the variable name)

Why this matters:
  - Public forks inherit the .md files but not your local env, so a leaked key in a markdown file ships to every fork
  - Pre-commit hooks scan for high-entropy strings in any file extension, not just .env
  - Rotating a key should be a one-line env update, never a commit + force-push
